title: Impulse
subtitle: Impulse and change in momentum for Class 11 Physics (NCERT)

blocks.heading: The Idea
blocks.body:
Impulse is the total effect of a force acting over time. The same change in momentum can be achieved with a big force for a short time, or a small force for a long time.
This is the secret behind every "soft landing": stretch the time, and you shrink the force.

blocks.heading: The Formula
blocks.body:
Impulse = force × time = change in momentum:
Symbols:
\vec{J} — impulse (N·s, same units as momentum)
\vec{F} — average force (N)
\Delta t — time the force acts (s)
\Delta\vec{p} — change in momentum (kg·m/s)
\vec{u},\ \vec{v} — initial and final velocity (m/s)

blocks.heading: Real-World Example
blocks.body:
A cricketer "draws the hands back" while catching a fast ball. The ball's momentum must drop to zero either way (`Δp` is fixed). By increasing the catching time `Δt`, the force `F = Δp / Δt` on the hands becomes small — so it doesn't sting.
The same physics is why cars have crumple zones and why we land on bent knees.

blocks.heading: Try It At Home
blocks.body: Drop a raw egg from the same small height onto (a) a hard plate and (b) a thick folded towel. The plate stops it instantly (tiny `Δt`, huge force → crack). The towel stretches the stopping time, lowering the force enough to survive. Same `Δp`, very different force.

blocks.heading: What If…
blocks.body: Airbags inflate in about 0.03 seconds. Why would inflating too slowly be useless, and inflating instantly hard also be dangerous? Think about what value of `Δt` you actually want.

quiz.question: Why does a high-jumper land on a thick foam mat instead of bare ground?
options: The mat reduces the change in momentum
The mat increases the stopping time, reducing the force
quiz.explanation: The change in momentum is the same. The mat increases Δt, so F = Δp/Δt is much smaller — a gentler stop.
